Kahraman, Oskay

coredata.dc:description: The present work reports the synthesis of graphene quantum dots with a green approach. Graphene quantum dots (ssGQDs) were synthesized via Sarcopoterium spinosum (L.) Spach. fruit powder extract. The prepared ssGQDs were characterized by UV-Vis Spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Malvern Zeta Sizer. The electrochemical activity of ssGQDs was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential puls voltammetry (DPV) methods. The synthesized ssGQDs were used to prepare electrosensor and tested for the electrochemical determination of dopamine. DPV studies showed a low detection limit of 0.107 μM over a wide linear concentration range of 10–800 μM. Antibacterial and antioxidant activities of synthesized ssGQDs were also investigated. The results showed that ssGQDs exhibited different antibacterial activity on Gram (+), Gram (-) bacterial strains and antifungal activity against fungal strains. 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH) hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) scavenging methods were used to examine the antioxidant activity of ssGQDs and were found to be effective.

CV curves of ssGQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS comprasing 1.0 mM DA at different scan rates (a), relationship between peak current and square root of scan rate (b), linear regression of anodic (Epa) and cathodic peak potential (Epc) against logarithmic scan rate (c), CV curves of DA recorded at different pH on ssGQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (d), and relationship between oxidation peak potentials and pH values (e).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The present work reports the synthesis of graphene quantum dots with a green approach. Graphene quantum dots (ssGQDs) were synthesized via Sarcopoterium spinosum (L.) Spach. fruit powder extract. The prepared ssGQDs were characterized by UV-Vis Spectroscopy, X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) and Malvern Zeta Sizer. The electrochemical activity of ssGQDs was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential puls voltammetry (DPV) methods. The synthesized ssGQDs were used to prepare electrosensor and tested for the electrochemical determination of dopamine. DPV studies showed a low detection limit of 0.107 μM over a wide linear concentration range of 10–800 μM. Antibacterial and antioxidant activities of synthesized ssGQDs were also investigated. The results showed that ssGQDs exhibited different antibacterial activity on Gram (+), Gram (-) bacterial strains and antifungal activity against fungal strains. 1,1-diphenyl-2-picryl-hydrazyl (DPPH) hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) scavenging methods were used to examine the antioxidant activity of ssGQDs and were found to be effective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters are physiologically significant class of biogenic amine molecules that can transmit electrical impulse between nerve cells. Dopamine (DA), an important member of neurotransmitters, is a hormone that controls memory and emotions. It plays a very important role in message transmission, regulation of hormonal level, central nervous, cardiovascular and renal systems [1]. Therefore, it is of great importance to monitor the DA level because abnormal DA secretion is associated with many health problems such as schizophrenia and Parkinson's disease [2]. With the enormous advances in nanotechnology, sensitive, stable and affordable biosensors have been developed using a variety of templates such as nanoparticles [3], carbon nanotubes [4] and graphene oxide [5]. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of the carbon family, are one of the materials that has attracted the attention of the scientific world. GQDs with a size below 10 nm have superior properties such as strong fluorescence [6,7], water well-solubility [8], bioharmoniousness [9], low toxicity [10,11]. It has also shown to have significant application potential in various field such as bioimaging [12], energy storage devices [13] and sensor [14,15]. In the literature review, it was seen that graphene quantum dots were synthesized in different ways [16–19]. The main methods used in the synthesis of GQDs are top-down and bottom-up approaches. While low quantum efficiency and low photocatalytic activity are observed in the GQDs obtained by the top-down method, the starting materials are quite toxic in the bottom-up methods, causing harmful consequences for the environment and human health [20]. For these reasons, it is very important to find alternative synthesis methods to obtain graphene quantum dots. Plant materials for producing graphene quantum dot are an excellent source as they are contain with carbon compounds.

Oxidative stress occurs as a result of excessive increase in reactive oxygen species [21]. Oxidative stress plays an important role of for diseases [22,23]. Antioxidants can eliminate or reduce reactive species to an appropriate level, thereby attenuating disease development. For this reason, it is very important to find and to use new effective antioxidants.

Bacteria are at the forefront of the formation of infections in the world. Antibiotics have become the treatment of choice for bacterial diseases because of their cost and effectiveness [24]. However, after a certain period of time, bacteria become resistant to antibiotics. The resistance of bacteria to drugs poses a major problem in both economic and health areas. This makes it important to search for new materials with antimicrobial properties. Graphene quantum dots may have the potential to be effective antimicrobial agents with their small size, large surface area and other properties [25].

S. spinosum plant, which is widespread in the Mediterranean region, has an important place in terms of ethnopharmacology [26,27]. It is known that different parts of S. spinosum are employed in folk medicine in the treatment of various diseases [27]. Also, the fruits of the S. spinosum has show antidiabetic activity [28].

In this study, ssGQDs were synthesized for the first time using the fruits of S. spinosum. The study aims to improve an ssGQD-based electrosensor for DA determination, as well as to investigate the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of synthesized ssGQDs.

All chemicals are of analytical quality and were used without any purification step. 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III), potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate, potassium chloride were obtained from Merck. Sodium phosphate monobasic, sodium phosphate dibasic, DA, uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA), acetaminophen, glucose, trichloroacetic acid (TCA), ferric chloride (FeCl3), nitro blue tetrazolium chloride (NBT), methionine, riboflavin, salysilic acid and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) were received from Sigma-Aldrich.

Structural characterization was actualized by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) (Rikagu diffractometer with CuKa, λ = 1.5406 Å), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (FEI Quanta 650 Feg), Transmissive Electron Microscope (TEM) (JEOL JEM 1011 Megaview III Camera iTEM Software), High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) (FEI TALOS F200S TEM 200 kV), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS, PHI 5000 VersaProbe). Electrochemical studies were performed using the CHI 660E Electrochemical Workstation, where GCE, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were working electrode, counter and reference electrode, respectively. The pH of the solutions were adjusted using Methrom model pH meter.

The S. spinosum was collected by Dr. Riza Binzet from Mersin (Location: Mersin University Campus, damp slopes and scrub area, 22 Jun 2021, 36° 47′ N 34° 21′ E, 100–120 m, Binzet 202148 ( Fig. 1). The plant was identified by Dr. Riza Binzet. The voucher specimens are deposited in the Mersin University Research Herbarium (MERA), Mersin, Turkey.
The gathered fruits were dehydrated at room temperature (25 °C) for a months. Then, the dehydrated fruits were grounded into powder using a grinder (Blender 8011ES Model HGB2WTS3 400 W).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs using fruits of S. spinosum
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 g fruit powder was transferred to the crucible and it was calcinated 350 °C for two hours. After cooled to the room temperature, the material was ground into powder with the help of a mortar. Distilled water (100 mL) was added to the ground sample and stirred at 90 °C for two hours under reflux. The solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. It was then strained using a 0.22 µm syringe filter to deport grand particles. Solid ssGQDs structure was obtained by lyophilizer, then transferred to vials for further studies and stored at room conditions.

A glassy carbon electrode modified with ssGQD was prepared for the electrochemical detection of DA. Herein, a drop-coating method was used to prepare the ssGQDs based electrode. To prepare the electrosensor, 5 mg ssGQDs material was dispersed in 1 mL of deionized water, mixed with magnetic stirrer for 24 h and then it was kept in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous mixture. 8 µL of the mixture was poured onto the electrode surface and left in an oven at 50° to evaporate the solvent. Finally, the ssGQDs modified electrode was plated with 5 µL of Nafion® 0.5 % (in alcohol) solution, so the modified electrode is up for electrochemical studies.

The antioxidant activity of ssGQDs was determined as in the previous study [29,30]. Various concentrations of samples (62.5, 125, 250, 500, 1000 µg/mL) were mixed with 2 mL of newly DPPH solution (1 mM) prepared in methanol. These mixtures were incubated at room temperature in the dark for one hour. After an incubation period of one hour, the absorbance of the sample was recorded at 517 nm. The antioxidant activity of ssGQDs was evaluated using the following equation.
(1) Radical scavenging activity % = [1 − (Cc/Cs)] * 100
Cs and Cc denote the DPPH radical with and without sample, respectively. Ascorbic acid and BHT was used as positive control.

Various concentration of the samples (40, 60, 80, 100 µg/mL) were mixed with 2 mL phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH 7.4) and 600 µL hydrogen peroxide solution (40 mM H2O2 in phosphate buffer, 0.1 M, pH 7.4). The mixture was vortexed and incubated for 10 min. Then the absorbance was measured at 230 nm [31]. The reaction mixture without samples serve as negative control while phosphate buffer was used as blank. Ascorbic acid was used as positive control. All solution was freshly prepared. The percentage of hydrogen peroxide scavenging activity was calculated using the following equation.
(2) % Scavenging activity = [(Ac − As)/Ac] * 100
Where, Ac and As refer to control and samples, respectively.

The reducing power activity of ssGQDs was investigated by the method of Oyaizu [32]. Various concentration of the samples (62.5, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 µg/mL), 2.5 mL of sodium phosphate buffer (0.2 M, pH 6.6) and 2.5 mL of 1 % potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) solution were added into the tubes. The reaction mixtures was incubated for 20 min at 50 °C. Then, 2.5 mL of 10 % trichloroacetic acid (TCA) solution was added into the tubes. The mixtures were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. Supernatant solutions (2.5 mL) were withdrawn from the tubes and was mixed with 2.5 mL of distilled water and 0.5 mL of 0.1 % ferric chloride (FeCl3) solution. Then, this mixtures was incubated for 10 min. The absorbance of the solution was measured at 700 nm using a spectrophotometer against blank.

The superoxide scavenging activity was investigated of the ssGQDs using the method reported by Martinez et al. [33]. 3 mL of the reaction mixture containing 13 mM methionine, 2 µM riboflavin, 75 µM NBT and 0.1 mM EDTA was taken and mixed with the samples at various concentrations (62.5, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 µg/mL). These mixtures were illuminated under a fluorescent lamp for 40 min. Absorbances were then measured at 560 nm. Ascorbic acid was used as positive control. The superoxide radical scavenging activity of samples was calculated by the Eq. (1).

The hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of the ssGQDs was determinated using the method reported by Ji et al. with some modification [34]. The hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of the ssGQDs was determined by the salysilic acid method. Various concentration of the samples (62.5, 125, 250, 500 and 1000 µg/mL) were mixed with 9 mM salicylic acid, 9 mM FeCl3 and 9 mM H2O2. The reaction mixtures were incubated for 40 min at 37 °C in a water bath. Then, the absorbance of the samples was measured at 510 nm. The hydroxyl radical scavenging activity of the samples was calculated by following equation.
(3) [(Asample – Ablank)/Acontrol] × 100

In this study, six reference bacterial strains (Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 29213, Streptococcus pneumoniae ATCC 49619, Enterococcus faecalis ATCC 29212, Escherichia coli ATCC 25922, Klebsiella pneumoniae ATCC BAA-1901, Pseudomonas aeruginosa ATCC 27853) and two fungal strains (Candida metapsilosis CBS 2916 CBS: KNAW, Candida parapsilosis ATCC 22019) were used. The antimicrobial susceptibility test was evaluated using the modification microdilution assay in 96-well sterile polystyrene microplates [35]. The bacterial and fungal cell inoculum was prepared from the stock culture grown in Mueller-Hinton Agar (MHA, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) at 37 °C for for 24 h and Tryptic Soy Agar (TSA, Merck, Darmstadt, Germany) at 28 °C for 24 h, respectively. The antimicrobial susceptibility test were performed as described by our previous study [30].

The optical property of the synthesized ssGQDs was analyzed by UV-Vis spectrophotometry. In the UV-Vis spectrum, two absorption bands were observed at 228 and 325 nm which is attributed the the π - π* transition of aromatic CC and n - π* transition of CO bonds, respectively ( Fig. 2) [36,37]. The inset in Fig. 2 shows the color of the ssGQDs under natural light and UV light at 365 nm.

Fig. 3 provides the XRD pattern for the synthesized ssGQDs. The intense peaks seen at 2θ = 28.30°, 40.49° can be attributed to the (002) and (100) planes of hexagonal graphite carbon structure, respectively, suggesting that ssGQDs were produced through carbonization of S. spinosum fruit [38,39].

The morphology, composition, average particle size and zeta potential of the synthesized ssGQDs were investigated by SEM, TEM, EDX and DLS. SEM micrograph of ssGQDs ( Fig. 4a) reveals its layered structure. SEM images show similarities with studies reported by Kir et al. and Önal et al. [40,41]. The layer appears to be separated from each other due to the presence of hydroxyl and epoxide groups in the basal plane. SEM images at different magnifications are given in the supplementary file (Fig. S1). The TEM image seen in Fig. 4b shows that the green synthesized ssGQDs are highly dispersed. HR-TEM analysis shows that the ssGQDs particles are monodisperse and have small sheet-shaped morphology (Fig. 4c,d). Those particles exhibit single-crystalline shaped nanocrystals with interlayer d-spacing of approximately 0.20 nm (inset of Fig. 4d). As shown in Fig. 4e, the sizes of the ssGQDs were mostly below 10 nm. The range of size distribution is very close and nearly 84 % of ssGQDs is in 4.2–6.5 nm. The elemental composition of ssGQDs was investigated by EDX spectroscopy. Carbon (88.58 %) and Oxygen (11.42 %) are the main elements of ssGQDs (Fig. 4f). The zeta potential gives information about the stability of the nanostructure in aqueous media. Nanomaterials that are well dispersed in aqueous media have zeta potential values less than − 30 mV or greater than + 30 mV and are deemed strong cationic or strong anionic [42]. The zeta potential of ssGQDs was determined as − 28.5 mV (Fig. 4g). The negative zeta potential of ssGQDs indicates that they are stable in aqueous media.

Further analysis were carried out to define the chemical composition of ssGQDs by XPS. The general survey spectra of the synthesized ssGQDs ( Fig. 5a). As seen in Fig. 5a, two characteristic peaks located at 288 eV and 534 eV were observed and assigned to C 1s and O 1s ssGQDs, respectively. High resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. 5b) show two distinct peaks at 285.4 eV and 287.6 eV which is attribute to C-O and CO, respectively [43]. The high resolution of O 1s (Fig. 5c) shows an intense peak at 533.7 eV, representing the CO band [44].

The electrochemical characterization of ssGQDs modified electrode was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) using K2[Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe. Fig. 6a depicts the current responses of the GCE and ssGQDs/GCE. As seen in Fig. 6a, the unmodified GCE shows a well-defined redox peak with a peak to peak separation of 120 mV while ssGQDs/GCE shows a poor redox peak with a large peak-to-peak separation. This result indicates that the electrode surface was successfully modified with ssGQDs. CV analyses were also carried out to determine the electrochemical behavior of DA on modified electrode in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.5) containing 1.0 mM DA at scan rate of 50 mV s−1. As seen in Fig. 6b, it is clear that the ssGQDs/GCE exhibit greater anodic peak current compared to GCE. This was probably due to the conductivity of ssGQDs that facilitate the electron transfer and enhanced the adsorption of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of potential scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para:
The CV curves of ssGQDs/GCE at various scan rates in the potential range of − 0.2 to 0.6 V in 0.1 M PBS containing 1.0 mM DA is shown in Fig. 7a. It is observed the the oxidation and reduction peak current increases with increasing scan rate. It was observed that the oxidation and reduction peak current increased with increasing scanning speed with a good linear relationship. The graph of the the square root of the scan rate versus current is given in Fig. 7b. Correlation coefficient values for oxidation and reduction peak current were determined as 0.991 and 0.995, respectively, demonstrates that the electrochemical process is diffusion-controlled. From the CV recorded at different scan rates, the number electron transferred during electrochemical process can also be determined. Based on the Laviron theory, the following equations can be derived for anodic (Ea) and cathodic (Ec) process [2,45].
(3)

Where α, n, R, T and F denote the electron transfer coefficient, transferred electron, universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), Kelvin temperature (298 K) and Faraday constant (96.500 C mol−1), respectively. According to Eqs. (3) and (4), there is a linear relationship between the redox potential and the logarithm of the scan rate. The linear regression equations are

for anodic and cathodic process, respectively (Fig. 7c). Therefore, the electron transfer coefficient (α) and number of electrons transferred ( n ) are determined to be 0.561 and 1.75, respectively.

The pH of the electrolyte is another important factor affecting the electrocatalytic reaction of DA. For this reason, CV was used to examine the effect of pH (in the range of 6.0–8.0) on the determination of DA at the ssGQDs/GCE surface (Fig. 7d). As seen in Fig. 7d, the oxidation current increases with increasing pH and the peak current is observed to reach a maximum at pH 7.5. Therefore, pH 7.5 was chosen as the optimum electrolyte medium for further experiments. From Fig. 7d, it is clearly that the oxidation peak potential of DA shift to less positive with increasing pH, demonstrating the direct involvement of the protons in the electrode reaction process [46]. Fig. 7e shows the variation of the peak potential with pH. As seen in Fig. 7e, there is a linear relationship between the peak potential and pH, with linear regression equation of
E
pa
= − 0.0627 pH + 0.799 .
The slope value of 0.0627 mV/pH is close to Nernst's theoretical value of 0.059 mV/pH, indicating that the number of protons and electrons in the electrocatalytic reaction of DA are equivalent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical determination of DA at ssGQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para:
Under optimum conditions, ssGQDs/GCE were used to investigate their effectiveness in the electrochemical determination of DA by differential puls voltammetry (DPV). DPV was recorded on the surface of ssGQDs/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.5) containing different concentration of DA ranging from 10 μM to 800 μM ( Fig. 8a). In Fig. 8b, it is seen that the electro-oxidation peak current increases linearly as the DA concentration increases with a linear regression equation of
I
μ A
= 33.25
C
m M
+ 9.95
R
2
= 0.995
.
The detection of limits (LOD) is calculated as 0.107 μM. It can be seen that the performance of the ssGQDs/GCE is superior to or approximately close to previously reported studies ( Table 1).

The reproducibility of the ssGQDs/GCE was evaluated by CV in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.5) containing 1.0 mM DA at scan rate of 50 mV/s. The reproducibility of the proposed sensor was examined by measuring the oxidation peak currents of four individually fabricated modified electrodes with relative standard deviation (RSD) of 4.13 % ( Fig. 9a). In addition, the selectivity of ssGQDs/GCE against DA was investigated in the presence of some interfering species such as glucose, uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA) and acetaminophen using amperometry technique under optimum experimental conditions (Fig. 9b). The amperometric response showed that the interfering species had no considerable effect, indicating that the ssGQDs/GCE exhibits well selectivity for electrochemical DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applicability of ssGQDs/GCE in real examples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of ssGQDs/GCE in the analysis of real samples, DA was determined in human urine by standard addition method in the linear concentration range of the sensor. The feasibility of the proposed sensor in the real sample was determined by its recovery rate. Prior to measurement, the urine samples were diluted 10 times with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.5) without any pretreatment (Fig. S2). DA concentration in human urine was determined using the linear regression equation in Fig. 8b. As tabulated in Table 2, recovery of urine samples was calculated in the range of 102.8–106.0 % and the RSDs were fewer than 4 %. These findings indicate that the electrode can be used for DA determination in real sample.

DPPH radical scavenging test method is widely used to estimate antioxidant activities due to the relatively short time required for analysis. Antioxidant properties of the samples on DPPH is due to their hydrogen donating ability. The DPPH reduction mechanism is based on the scavenging of DPPH by hydrogen atom transfer from functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups in the structure of ssGQDs [54]. Antioxidant activity of the GQDs depend to the synthesis route of GQDs, H- donor ability, content of the surface oxygen groups and type of the oxygen groups (hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxyl). GQDs have sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms. Therefore GQDs have a large amount of oxygen groups at edge and basal plane. Each type of oxygen group has a different hydrogen donating or electron transfer ability [55]. Free radical can form at the edges and defects of sites on the graphitic plane. Surface oxygen groups of GQDs can provide high reaction activity on defect sites [56]. Our results showed that the synthesized ssGQDs has moderate antioxidant activity. Ascorbic acid showed greater DPPH free radical inhibition than ssGQDs at all concentrations used. The IC50 value are concentration required to reduce 50 % of the DPPH free radical. The IC50 values for ssGQDs and ascorbic acid were determinated as 162.58 mg/mL and 1.97 mg/mL, respectively ( Fig. 10a).

Zhao et al. synthesized GQDs by using three different electrolytes. In this study, it was determined that the DPPH scavenging activity of GQDs-NaOH GQDs-PBS and GQDs-KCl reached 79.55 %, 52.80 % and 46.89 %, respectively, in 5 h [57]. In another study, the DPPH radical scavenging activity of synthesized GQDs was determined as 79 % [58]. Wang et al. determined that the DPPH scavenging activity of four different GQD samples after 24 h of incubation was 46.58 % (GQDs), 40.93 % (rGQDs-1), 34.12 % (rGQDs-2) and 25.98 % (rGQDs-3) [55]. According to our results, it was determined that the synthesized ssGQDs showed a maximum of 50 % DPPH scavenging activity after one hour of incubation.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is one of the inducers of aging and attacks cellular energy-producing systems [59]. H2O2 is non toxic at low amount. However, H2O2 can take part in the formation of hydroxyl radical through various reactions. Hydroxyl radical is highly reactive species and it has toxic potential at cell. In this paper the ssGQDs and ascorbic acid showed concentration-dependent H2O2 scavenging activities, as showed in Fig. 10b. At 100 µg/mL concentration, H2O2 scavenging activity was determined as 60.19 % and 70.70 %, and IC50 values were determined as 0.186 mg/mL and 0.129 mg/mL, for ssGQDs and ascorbic acid, respectively.

An important test in elucidating the antioxidant capacity of the samples is the determination of their reduction potential. This test is based on measuring the absorbance of the samples at 700 nm and the increase in the absorbance value indicates an increase in the reducing power. The reducing power activity of ssGQDs show in Fig. 11a. Ascorbic acid has showed strong reducing power than ssGQDs at all concentration.

Superoxide radical scavenging activity was measured based by degradation of the riboflavin at fluorescent lamps. As a result of the degradation of riboflavin under light, superoxide radicals are formed [60]. At concentration 1000 µg/mL ssGQDs and ascorbic acid showed inhibition to 41.90 % and 52.70 %, respectively (Fig. 11b).

Among the reactive oxygen species, the hydroxyl radical is the most reactive and ability to easily oxidize various biological macromolecules with high oxidation efficiency and fast reaction speed [61] The hydroxyl radical causes to death blood cells and degrades cellular components such as DNA and macromolecules [62] In this study, ssGQDs showed high hydroxyl radical scavenging activity than ascorbic acid at all concentration (Fig. 11c). At concentration 1000 µg/mL ssGQDs and ascorbic acid showed inhibition to 56.74 % and 51.44 %, respectively. Zhao et al. reported that the hydroxyl radical scavenging activity for GQDs-NaOH, GQDs-PBS and GQDs-KCl after one hour of reaction was 68.05 %, 54.55 % and 30.11 %, respectively [63].

Antifungal and antibacterial activities of ssGQDs synthesized in this study were also investigated. MIC of the ssGQDs has ranging from 125 to 250 µg/mL against all tested bacteria. The difference antimicrobial activity of ssGQDs may be due to the bacterial membrane structure. Furthermore, the functional groups in ssGQDs and the surface charge between strains can affect the antimicrobial activity. Also ssGQDs has the MIC value at 62.5 µg/mL especially against increasingly important pathogens as C. parapsilosis and C. metapsilosis ( Table 3).

Kadian et al. reported that graphene quantum dot nanocomposites have an MIC of > 750 µg/mL against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa bacteria [64]. In another study by Biswas et al., the antimicrobial activities of GQDs were investigated and they stated that GQDs have a MIC value of 512 µg/mL against S. aureus NCIM 5021 and P. aeruginosa NCIM 5029 [65]. Liang et al., reported that the carbon dots have MICs value of 64, 128, 64, and 128 μg/mL for E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and B. subtilis respectively [66]. Marković et al. noted that the antimicrobial activities of GQDs have MIC values of 15.62, 62.5, 62.5, 125 and 250 µg/mL for K. pneumoniae, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus and E. faecalis [67]. In this study, the MIC value of the obtained ssGQDs has ranging from 62.5 to 250 µg/mL against all tested bacteria.

In this study, we synthesized ssGQDs via simple, economic and non toxic synthesis route using S. spinosum fruit. Green synthesized ssGQDs were used as a platform to develop a ssGQD-based electrosensor for the determination of DA. as well as to investigate the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of synthesized ssGQDs. The electrochemical behavior of the ssGODs/GCE was searched by CV and DPV methods. The results demonstrated that developed electrosensor owned great electrochemical performance with the inclusion of sensitivity, selectivity and low detection limit. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 0.107 µM in the concentration range of 10–800 µM. The antioxidant ability of biosynthesized ssGQDs was also examined. The antioxidant investigation showed that ssGQDs had a significant DPPH and H2O2 free radical scavenging activity. Furthermore, antifungal and antibacterial activities of ssGQDs synthesized were investigated. The results showed that the MIC values of the ssGQDs ranged from 125 to 250 µg/mL against all bacteria tested. As a result, it can be concluded that ssGQDs show superior electrocatalytic activity, significant antioxidant antimicrobial activity.

Ersan Turunc: Resources, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Oskay Kahraman: Synthesis, characterization and writing draft. Aylin Dogen: Contibuted to antimicrobial studies. Riza Binzet: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
